questions.prompt: Why has trunk-based development (GitHub Flow) replaced git-flow for most teams?

choices.text: Less merge pain (short branches diverge less from main), faster feedback (integration happens daily), simpler mental model (one branch to deploy from), better fit for CI/CD and continuous deployment, simpler for monorepos. Git-flow's multiple long-lived branches (main, develop, release/*, hotfix/*) accumulate divergence and ceremony — every merge step is a decision point, and the coordination overhead becomes disproportionate for teams shipping rapidly. Vincent Driessen (git-flow's creator) himself wrote in 2020 that git-flow doesn't fit modern continuous-delivery teams. Git-flow still fits versioned products, regulated environments, or coordinated quarterly releases — but those are minority cases now.

questions.explanation: Less merge pain, faster feedback, simpler model, better CI/CD fit. Git-flow is too much ceremony for most.

questions.prompt: Team wants to ship incomplete features without long-running branches. Pattern?

choices.text: Feature flags. Merge incomplete code to main behind a flag: `if (featureFlag('new-checkout'))`. The code ships; the flag determines who sees it. Roll out gradually (0% → 5% → 25% → 100%); flip off if broken; A/B test variants. Decouples DEPLOY (code goes to production) from RELEASE (users see it). With feature flags, long-lived feature branches become unnecessary — you merge daily, behind a flag, activate when ready. Tools: LaunchDarkly, GrowthBook, Statsig, or simple custom flag systems. Foundation for trunk-based at scale; lets you keep main always-deployable while features develop in code.

questions.explanation: Feature flags decouple deploy from release. Merge incomplete features behind flags; activate later.
